gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of identifying pathogenic amino acid variants and understanding their molecular mechanisms. Existing methods have high false-positive rates and do not provide actionable hypotheses about molecular consequences. MutPred2 extends MutPred1 to improve pathogenicity prioritization and probabilistically model variant impact on specific protein structural and functional properties, enabling mechanism-driven disease studies.

section_gists.heading: Inferring the molecular and phenotypic impact of amino acid variants with MutPred2
section_gists.gist: Introduces MutPred2 as an improved tool for prioritizing pathogenic amino acid substitutions and generating molecular mechanisms. Highlights shortcomings of existing methods: inadequate balance of false-positive/true-positive rates and lack of actionable molecular hypotheses. MutPred2 models a broad repertoire of structural and functional alterations from sequence. It is validated on Mendelian and neurodevelopmental disorder variants, with experimental validation of selected predictions.

Need for improved prioritization and mechanistic insights
Experimental validation planned for high-scoring variants
methods: Probabilistic modeling of variant impact on protein properties
Sequence-based predictors for structural and functional properties
failures_or_limits: Existing methods have high false-positive rates
Existing methods do not provide molecular mechanism hypotheses

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes MutPred2's design: a bagged ensemble of neural networks trained on pathogenic and unlabeled variants. Addresses challenges: integrating disparate data, unifying predictor implementations, handling unequal prior probabilities of properties, and interrelations among properties. Develops >50 property predictors within a positive-unlabeled learning framework, transforms scores to posterior probabilities, and constructs an ontology of molecular alterations. Evaluates pathogenicity model on independent test sets, compares favorably to existing tools. Estimates fraction of pathogenic variants in personal genomes and identifies molecular signatures in disease datasets. Experimentally validates functional impact of several variants in neurodevelopmental disorders.

Use of positive-unlabeled learning to handle unlabeled data
Construction of ontology to organize molecular consequences

Comparison with ACMG/AMP recommended tools on independent test sets

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: MutPred2 improves pathogenicity prediction and assigns molecular alterations, aiding experimental validation. Estimates ~100 heterozygous and 25 homozygous pathogenic variants per individual. Notes that filtering training data by MAF is not always beneficial; model trained on full data generalizes well. Acknowledges noise in training sets but suggests small impact. Predicts more pathogenic de novo mutations in neurodevelopmental disorder cases than controls, without using brain-specific features. Provides examples of plausible mechanistic predictions (CHD8, RYR3). Proposes a bottom-up, mechanism-driven approach to disease study. Limitations: does not account for epistatic interactions, bias in training sets, and some predictions may be difficult to interpret (e.g., altered properties).

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Details input (sequence and substitution), output (pathogenicity score and molecular mechanisms). Training data: 53,180 pathogenic (HGMD, SwissVar, dbSNP) and 206,946 unlabeled (Swiss-Prot, dbSNP, interspecies alignments) variants. Features: 1345 features in six groups (sequence, substitution, PSSM, conservation, homolog profiles, changes in predicted properties). Feature selection via t-test (p<0.01), PCA retaining 99% variance. Ensemble of 30 neural networks (single hidden layer, 4 neurons) trained with bagging on balanced samples. Training stopped by validation set performance. Molecular mechanisms inferred from property predictors: loss/gain probabilities computed from wt and mt sequences; property score is max of loss/gain posterior; baseline threshold 0.25. Property predictors developed in positive-unlabeled framework with posterior calibration.

events.summary: Existing pathogenicity prediction methods have major shortcomings: inadequate balance of false-positive and true-positive rates and lack of actionable molecular mechanism hypotheses.

MutPred2 is designed as a machine learning method that provides a general pathogenicity prediction and a ranked list of specific molecular alterations, using positive-unlabeled learning and posterior probabilities.

To address challenges in variant interpretation, over 50 sequence-based property predictors were developed within a positive-unlabeled framework, with scores transformed to posterior probabilities and organized into an ontology.
The MutPred2 pathogenicity model was trained on 53,180 pathogenic and 206,946 unlabeled variants using a bagged ensemble of feed-forward neural networks.
methods_or_tools: bagged ensemble of feed-forward neural networks

events.summary: Evaluation of different training set filtering criteria showed that using the entire unfiltered training set resulted in comparable or better performance, so no filtering was performed.
Cross-validation AUC was 87.7%, corrected to 91.3% after estimating label noise (2.8% noisy positives, 5.8% pathogenic in unlabeled).
Conservation-based features were most discriminative; predicted conservation features improved performance by two percentage points over no conservation features.
Excluding homology profiles did not drastically affect performance when true conservation features were available, but decreased AUC by two percentage points otherwise.

events.summary: Evaluated MutPred2 against MutPred on its original training set, finding MutPred2 AUC of 88.0%, outperforming MutPred by about five percentage points.
MutPred2 achieved highest AUC (87.1%) on the independent set, with high sensitivity at low false-positive rates, outperforming CADD at lower FPRs.
Relaxed sequence identity threshold to 80% to expand dataset, confirming general trends but noting SIFT's reduced performance.
Analyzed score distributions on healthy genomes, finding MutPred2 scores better suited for interpretation as probabilities, with stronger negative correlation with MAFs.
Estimated proportions of pathogenic missense variants in training data and healthy genomes using AlphaMax, finding 5.8% of unlabeled variants may be pathogenic.
Identified enriched molecular mechanisms in Mendelian diseases, recapitulating structural changes and finding enrichment for metal binding, allosteric regulation, and macromolecular binding sites.
Applied MutPred2 to de novo mutations in neurodevelopmental disorders, finding significantly higher proportions of pathogenic mutations in cases than controls at 10% and 5% FPR thresholds.

Experimental validation of MutPred2 predictions using yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) to test impact of high-scoring mutations on protein-protein interactions.
MutPred2 predictions agreed with experimental observations for most tested mutations, with one exception (A502T in ZBTB18).
Caution that loss of PPIs may not be the primary pathogenic mechanism; other mechanisms like loss of stability or aberrant folding could be involved.
Estimates do not account for epistatic interactions such as compensatory variants.
MutPred2 enables a bottom-up, mechanism-driven approach to studying genetic disease, grouping diseases by molecular mechanisms.

source.title: Inferring the molecular and phenotypic impact of amino acid variants with MutPred2

initial_request.research_problem: Existing pathogenicity prediction methods have high false-positive rates and do not provide actionable molecular mechanism hypotheses.
initial_request.target_object: MutPred2: a machine learning method for predicting pathogenicity and molecular mechanisms of amino acid substitutions
initial_request.known_context: Current methods are inadequate for exome-scale applications due to suboptimal balance of false-positive and true-positive rates and lack of mechanistic insights.

evidence.quote_or_span: they remain inadequate to the task in exome-scale applications owing to a less than optimal balance of false-positive and true-positive detection rates
they do not generate actionable hypotheses regarding the molecular consequences of these variants
MutPred2 is a machine learning-based method, and software package that integrates genetic and molecular data to reason probabilistically about the pathogenicity of amino acid substitutions.
This is achieved by providing (1) a general pathogenicity prediction, and (2) a ranked list of specific molecular alterations potentially affecting the phenotype.
